For a given RSU r, we define gr ∈ {0, 1} to indicate whether it is equipped with a GPU, while gr = 1 represents that RSU r is equipped with a GPU. Furthermore, the storage resource of RSU r is denoted as smaxr, we use rcpu and frgpu to represent the available computation resources of its CPU and GPU, respectively. Each RSU has a task queue, where tasks are processed in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) manner.

Let V = {v | v = 1, 2, · · · , V } denote the set of vehicles. The vehicle generates a number of inference tasks (e.g., path planning, pedestrian detection, traffic light detection, etc.). Since the limited computational resources, vehicles cannot process inference tasks locally. Instead, they offload these tasks to RSUs within their communication range for execution.

# 3.2 Details for DNN Inference

It is important to note that RSUs must have the corresponding DNN models to execute inference tasks. We use M = {m | m = 1, 2, · · · , M} to denote the set of all types of DNN models, with each inference request corresponding to one of these models for execution. We consider each DNN model to have three versions with different levels of inference accuracy (low, medium, and high). Let P = {p | p = 1, 2, 3} indicate three accuracy levels, where the elements of the set correspond to low, medium, and high inference accuracy, respectively. Then, we use πmp to represent the mth model with accuracy level p.

For a particular inference request, there is a trade-off between response delay and inference accuracy for using different models to serve this request. To achieve lower inference latency may sacrifice the corresponding inference accuracy. Thus, choosing the best inference model is a major challenge. Due to the resource constraints of RSUs, we assume that for each type of model, an RSU can deploy at most one accuracy level model of that model type. The workload required for inference by the model πmp is denoted as wcpu for the CPU and wgpu for the GPU, the storage space required to store the model is cmp, where m ∈ M and p ∈ P. Without loss of generality, higher inference accuracy models require more storage space and longer inference times.

Based on the above premises, we define the integer DNN model caching variable as xmpr ∈ {0, 1} (r ∈ R, m ∈ M, p ∈ P). Specifically, xmpr can be defined as,

(1) xmpr =

1, if model πmp is deployed on RSU r,

0, otherwise.

Let X = {xmpr | r ∈ R, m ∈ M, p ∈ P}. This records all deployment decisions for DNN models.

We use A = {Aj | j ∈ R} to represent all inference requests received by the RSUs in the system, where Aj denotes the set of all requests received by RSU j. Let Aj = {ajk | j ∈ R, k ∈ Nj}, where ajk is an inference request received by RSU j. Here, Nj = {k | k = 1, 2, · · · , nj}, and nj denote the total
